region_of_origin: Global (walnuts originate from Central Asia; widely consumed internationally)

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general walnut entry; no specific baked-and-salted variant exists. Closest defensible match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LC covers walnuts as a category; no sub-code for baked-salted preparation. Closest defensible code used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT substance code for walnut used; no specific baked-salted preparation code exists.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical single serving of salted baked walnuts is approximately 30g (a small handful), providing around 193 kcal, 4.3g protein, 18.2g fat, and 193mg sodium.

nutrient_source.title: Baked walnut, salted — HPB Singapore Food Composition Database (Foodo)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for 'Baked walnut, salted' (CR ID: F09030019). All per-100g macro and micronutrient values are sourced from this record. Saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, and trans fat values supplemented from USDA FoodData Central (FDC ID 170187, English walnuts, dry-roasted, with salt) where HPB does not report fatty acid breakdown.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No GI value exists specifically for baked salted walnuts. GI of 15 is derived from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for walnuts (raw/roasted), consistent with Atkinson et al. (2008) Diabetes Care 31(12):2281-2283, which reports walnuts GI ≈ 15. The salting process does not materially alter glycemic response. GL per 30g serving calculated as: (15 × 17.86g carbohydrate per 100g × 30g serving) / 100 = 0.80.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content 643mg per 100g — exceeds the 600mg/100g threshold for hypertension concern
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit salted baked walnuts due to the high sodium content of 643mg/100g; unsalted walnuts are strongly preferred to retain cardiovascular benefits without the sodium burden, and if salted walnuts are consumed, portions should be restricted to no more than 15g per occasion.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low glycemic index (GI ≈ 15), low sugar (3.57g/100g), high dietary fibre (7.1g/100g), and high PUFA content supporting insulin sensitivity
type2_diabetes.guidance: Salted baked walnuts are suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to their very low glycemic index, low sugar content, and high fibre and healthy fat profile that supports insulin sensitivity; however, portion size should be limited to approximately 30g per serving given the high caloric density of 643 kcal/100g.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High polyunsaturated fat (43.48g/100g, predominantly ALA omega-3) is cardioprotective, but high sodium (643mg/100g) is a cardiovascular risk factor
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Walnuts provide substantial cardiovascular benefit through their high alpha-linolenic acid (ALA, omega-3) and PUFA content, which reduce LDL cholesterol and inflammation; however, the added salt at 643mg sodium per 100g is a concern for cardiovascular disease management, and unsalted walnuts are preferred, with salted variants limited to small portions of 15–20g.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Rich source of alpha-linolenic acid (ALA, omega-3) which reduces joint inflammation; dominant MUFA/PUFA profile qualifies for whole-nut clinical carve-out
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Salted baked walnuts are suitable for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis given their high ALA omega-3 content that directly reduces joint inflammation; limit to approximately 30g per day and prefer unsalted variants for overall health.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Baked walnut, salted, is a minimally processed snack nut consisting of whole or halved walnuts (Juglans regia) that have been oven-baked or dry-roasted until light brown and crisp, then seasoned with salt. The result is a crunchy, toasted nut with a rich, nutty flavour and a savoury finish from the added salt. Per 100g, this food provides 643 kcal, 14.29g protein, 60.71g total fat (predominantly polyunsaturated fatty acids including alpha-linolenic acid, an omega-3 fatty acid), 17.86g carbohydrate, 3.57g sugar, 7.1g dietary fibre, and 643mg sodium. The high PUFA content — particularly ALA — makes walnuts one of the richest plant-based sources of omega-3 fatty acids, supporting cardiovascular health, reducing LDL cholesterol, and attenuating joint inflammation in rheumatoid arthritis. The glycemic index is very low (GI ≈ 15), making this food suitable for blood glucose management in Type 2 Diabetes when consumed in controlled portions of approximately 30g. The primary clinical caution is the high sodium content (643mg/100g), which exceeds the threshold for hypertension suitability; individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease should choose unsalted walnuts. Walnuts are a tree nut allergen and are vegan, gluten-free, and dairy-free. They are commonly consumed as a snack or added to breakfast dishes, salads, and baked goods.
common_user_queries: Are salted walnuts good for diabetics?
Can I eat baked walnuts if I have high blood pressure?
How many calories in salted walnuts?
Are walnuts good for heart health?
What is the sodium content of salted walnuts?
Are baked walnuts high in fat?
Are walnuts good for rheumatoid arthritis?
What is the glycemic index of walnuts?

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient and micronutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (CR ID: F09030019), an approved primary source with confidence 0.95. Fatty acid breakdown supplemented from USDA FoodData Central (FDC ID 170187, English walnuts, dry-roasted, with salt), an approved secondary source. GI value derived from University of Sydney GI Database via Atkinson et al. (2008), an approved GI source.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (5.72g/100g), monounsaturated_fat_g (8.93g/100g), polyunsaturated_fat_g (43.48g/100g), and trans_fat_g (0.0g/100g) are sourced from USDA FoodData Central FDC ID 170187 (Walnuts, English, dry roasted, with salt added) as HPB Foodo does not report fatty acid sub-fractions for this entry. Trans fat is confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central. 2. Vitamin C, vitamin A, B-vitamins (B1, B2, B3, B6, B12), folate, vitamin D, vitamin E, phosphorus, magnesium, zinc, selenium, and water_g are set to -1 (data not reported) as these micronutrient values are not available in the HPB Foodo entry for this specific baked-salted walnut record, and cross-referencing USDA FDC 170187 confirms these fields are either not reported or not applicable to this specific preparation in a directly comparable form; detailed micronutrient data should be sourced from USDA FDC 170187 if required. 3. Cholesterol is 0.0mg/100g — confirmed zero per HPB Foodo and USDA FoodData Central; walnuts are a plant food and naturally contain no cholesterol. 4. The description in the input JSON states 'Unsalted baked walnuts' which is inconsistent with the food name 'Baked walnut, salted' and the sodium value of 643mg/100g; the name and nutrient data are treated as authoritative, and the description is interpreted as referring to the base walnut prior to salting. 5. GI of 15 is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. (2008) for walnuts; no direct GI measurement for baked salted walnuts was found in approved sources. GL per 30g serving = (15 × 17.86g carbohydrate per 100g × 30g serving) / 100 = 0.80. 6. The 'wholegrain' dietary tag in the input is not applicable to walnuts and has been omitted from classification.
